Motion For Reconsideration Of The Court'S April 23, 2024 Orders
Off Calendar May-30-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF TRUCONNECT COMMUNICATIONS, INC.'s Motion For Reconsideration Of The Court'S April 23, 2024 Orders.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's "motion for reconsideration of the court's April 23, 2024 orders" on three pleadings motions is denied.

California law requires a meet-and-confer before a pleadings motion at which the party that filed the pleading "shall provide legal support for its position that the pleading is legally sufficient or, in the alternative, how" the pleading "could be amended to cure any legal insufficiency." (CCP 430.41(a)(1), 435.5(a)(1).) It was clear from counsel's declaration filed with the three motions that the law was not followed here (2/13/24 Perez Dec. 1:14-20) - and plaintiff's opposition papers on the three motions did not claim it was.

Thus, the court's tentative rulings posted April 22 (1) pointed out the illegality, (2) took the pleadings motions off calendar so the parties could comply with the meet-and-confer laws and (3) set a new date of May 23 for responsive papers. On April 23, the tentatives became the court's orders after no one contested them.
Plaintiff provides no valid reason for not presenting its arguments earlier. (CCP 1008; Gilberd v. AC Transit (1995) 32 Cal.App.4th 1494, 1500.) In any event, plaintiff's untimely arguments are unavailing. It is not the parties' role to decide when the meet-and-confer laws have been followed, it is the court's. (See Memo. 7:8-12.) Plaintiff had a "meaningful opportunity" to address the meet-and-confer at the April 23 hearing, but elected not to do so. (See id. at 7:13.) Plaintiff is not suffering "extraordinary prejudice" from the May 23 response date (see id. at 8:2-3); if the parties could not resolve their pleadings disputes in a compliant meet-and-confer (as they should), a hearing can be scheduled in mid-June - a few days' difference in a case filed in 2021.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling with a cover letter stating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)
